content:
Hello memory agent, you are continuing to observe the primary Claude session.

<observed_from_primary_session>
  <user_request>commit</user_request>
  <requested_at>2026-06-11</requested_at>
</observed_from_primary_session>

You are a Claude-Mem, a specialized observer tool for creating searchable memory FOR FUTURE SESSIONS.

CRITICAL: Record what was LEARNED/BUILT/FIXED/DEPLOYED/CONFIGURED, not what you (the observer) are doing.

You do not have access to tools. All information you need is provided in <observed_from_primary_session> messages. Create observations from what you observe - no investigation needed.

Your job is to monitor a different Claude Code session happening RIGHT NOW, with the goal of creating observations and progress summaries as the work is being done LIVE by the user. You are NOT the one doing the work - you are ONLY observing and recording what is being built, fixed, deployed, or configured in the other session.

SPATIAL AWARENESS: Tool executions include the working directory (tool_cwd) to help you understand:
- Which repository/project is being worked on
- Where files are located relative to the project root
- How to match requested paths to actual execution paths

WHAT TO RECORD
--------------
Focus on deliverables and capabilities:
- What the system NOW DOES differently (new capabilities)
- What shipped to users/production (features, fixes, configs, docs)
- Changes in technical domains (auth, data, UI, infra, DevOps, docs)

Use verbs like: implemented, fixed, deployed, configured, migrated, optimized, added, refactored

✅ GOOD EXAMPLES (describes what was built):
- "Authentication now supports OAuth2 with PKCE flow"
- "Deployment pipeline runs canary releases with auto-rollback"
- "Database indexes optimized for common query patterns"

❌ BAD EXAMPLES (describes observation process - DO NOT DO THIS):
- "Analyzed authentication implementation and stored findings"
- "Tracked deployment steps and logged outcomes"
- "Monitored database performance and recorded metrics"

WHEN TO SKIP
------------
Skip routine operations:
- Empty status checks
- Package installations with no errors
- Simple file listings
- Repetitive operations you've already documented
- If file related research comes back as empty or not found
- **No output necessary if skipping.**

IMPORTANT: Continue generating observations from tool use messages using the XML structure below.

OUTPUT FORMAT
-------------
Output observations using this XML structure:

```xml
<observation>
  <type>[ bugfix | feature | refactor | change | discovery | decision ]</type>
  <!--
    **type**: MUST be EXACTLY one of these 6 options (no other values allowed):
      - bugfix: something was broken, now fixed
      - feature: new capability or functionality added
      - refactor: code restructured, behavior unchanged
      - change: generic modification (docs, config, misc)
      - discovery: learning about existing system
      - decision: architectural/design choice with rationale
  -->
  <title>[**title**: Short title capturing the core action or topic]</title>
  <subtitle>[**subtitle**: One sentence explanation (max 24 words)]</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>[Concise, self-contained statement]</fact>
    <fact>[Concise, self-contained statement]</fact>
    <fact>[Concise, self-contained statement]</fact>
  </facts>
  <!--
    **facts**: Concise, self-contained statements
Each fact is ONE piece of information
      No pronouns - each fact must stand alone
      Include specific details: filenames, functions, values

**files**: All files touched (full paths from project root)
  -->
  <narrative>[**narrative**: Full context: What was done, how it works, why it matters]</narrative>
  <concepts>
    <concept>[knowledge-type-category]</concept>
    <concept>[knowledge-type-category]</concept>
  </concepts>
  <!--
    **concepts**: 2-5 knowledge-type categories. MUST use ONLY these exact keywords:
      - how-it-works: understanding mechanisms
      - why-it-exists: purpose or rationale
      - what-changed: modifications made
      - problem-solution: issues and their fixes
      - gotcha: traps or edge cases
      - pattern: reusable approach
      - trade-off: pros/cons of a decision

    IMPORTANT: Do NOT include the observation type (change/discovery/decision) as a concept.
    Types and concepts are separate dimensions.
  -->
  <files_read>
    <file>[path/to/file]</file>
    <file>[path/to/file]</file>
  </files_read>
  <files_modified>
    <file>[path/to/file]</file>
    <file>[path/to/file]</file>
  </files_modified>
</observation>
```


IMPORTANT! DO NOT do any work right now other than generating this OBSERVATIONS from tool use messages - and remember that you are a memory agent designed to summarize a DIFFERENT claude code session, not this one.

Never reference yourself or your own actions. Do not output anything other than the observation content formatted in the XML structure above. All other output is ignored by the system, and the system has been designed to be smart about token usage. Please spend your tokens wisely on useful observations.

Remember that we record these observations as a way of helping us stay on track with our progress, and to help us keep important decisions and changes at the forefront of our minds! :) Thank you so much for your help!

MEMORY PROCESSING CONTINUED
===========================

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.thinking:
Now I can see the actual changes. Let me analyze what was done:

1. The diff shows changes to `WantedPosterManager.ts`
2. The pattern is consistent across three locations in the file (lines 327, 396, and 487)
3. In each location, the order of operations was changed:
   - BEFORE: `SetWantedPosterData(data)` → `node.active = true`
   - AFTER: `node.active = true` → `SetWantedPosterData(data)`
4. A Chinese comment was added explaining WHY: "先 active 再 setSkin：inactive 狀態下 setSkin 不會重建已渲染骨架的 render data（殘留舊 skin）"
   - Translation: "First activate then setSkin: in inactive state, setSkin won't rebuild the rendered skeleton's render data (leaving old skin residue)"

This is clearly a BUGFIX. The issue was:
- Problem: When setSkin is called on an inactive node, it doesn't rebuild the skeleton's render data, causing old skin data to persist
- Solution: Activate the node first (set node.active = true), then call SetWantedPosterData which internally calls setSkin

The type should be "bugfix" because this fixes a rendering issue where old skin data would persist.

Let me structure the observation:

Type: bugfix
Title: Fixed skeleton skin rendering order in WantedPosterManager
Subtitle: Nodes must be activated before setting skin data to prevent old skeleton render data from persisting
Facts:
- WantedPosterManager.ts had SetWantedPosterData called before node.active = true in three methods
- Setting skin on inactive nodes doesn't rebuild skeleton render data, causing old skin residue
- Order changed to activate node first, then call SetWantedPosterData in ShowFirstBounty, PlayBounties, and EnterAt
Narrative: The Wanted Poster system had a rendering issue where old skeleton skin graphics persisted when new skins were applied. This happened because SetWantedPosterData was called before activating the node—in Cocos Creator's spine system, setSkin on inactive nodes skips rebuilding the render data. The fix reorders operations in three methods to activate the node first, then apply the new skin data, ensuring proper skeleton render data reconstruction.

Concepts: solution, gotcha, how-it-works

The solution involved reordering method calls in ShowFirstBounty, PlayBounties callback, and EnterAt to set node.active = true before calling SetWantedPosterData. The gotcha is that Cocos Creator's spine skeleton system requires the node to be active when setSkin is called for render data to rebuild properly. Modified assets/Script/WantedPoster/WantedPosterManager.ts to implement these changes.

content.text:
<observation>
  <type>bugfix</type>
  <title>Fixed skeleton skin rendering order in WantedPosterManager</title>
  <subtitle>Nodes must be activated before setting skin data to prevent old skeleton render data from persisting</subtitle>
  <facts>
    <fact>WantedPosterManager.ts called SetWantedPosterData before node.active = true in three methods</fact>
    <fact>Setting skin on inactive nodes doesn't rebuild skeleton render data, causing old skin residue</fact>
    <fact>Order changed to activate node first, then call SetWantedPosterData in ShowFirstBounty, PlayBounties, and EnterAt methods</fact>
  </facts>
  <narrative>Fixed a rendering bug in the Wanted Poster system where skeleton skin data would persist incorrectly. The issue occurred because SetWantedPosterData (which internally calls setSkin on spine skeletons) was being called before the node was activated. In Cocos Creator's spine skeleton system, calling setSkin on an inactive node doesn't rebuild the render data for the already-rendered skeleton, causing old skin graphics to remain visible. The fix reorders the operations in three methods (ShowFirstBounty at line 327, PlayBounties callback at line 396, and EnterAt at line 487) to first set node.active = true, then call SetWantedPosterData, ensuring the skeleton's render data is properly rebuilt with the new skin.</narrative>
  <concepts>

- For recycled items that had already rendered old skin, render data wasn't rebuilt, leaving intro's M1 residue
- Fix: Activate first, then setSkin

Claude's Full Response to User:
Commit 乾淨了 ✅(`00dacb3`,在 main 上)。訊息已修正,無 `@` 殘留。

## 收尾總結

- **問題**:FG 第一手懸賞令 BaseSymbol 9 顯示成 1(第二次正確)。
- **根因**:`node.active=false` 時呼叫 Spine `setSkin()`,對已渲染過舊 skin 的回收 item 不重建 render data。
- **修法**:`WantedPosterManager.ts` 三處(`enterAll`/`EnterAt`/`HandleGameIntroComplete`)一致化為「先 active 再 setSkin」。
- **驗證**:實機 log + 截圖確認 M9+M7 正確顯示。
- **commit**:`00dacb3`(1 file, +6/-3)。

未 push(依規矩等你決定)。需要的話我可以幫你 push,或把這次教訓(「Cocos sp.Skeleton:inactive node 上 setSkin 不刷新已渲染骨架」)寫進記憶供之後參考——但那條算可記可不記,你說了算。

content.thinking:
The user wants me to write a progress summary of what was observed in the primary Claude session. Let me review what happened:

1. The primary session was working on a bug in the WantedPosterManager where skeleton skins weren't refreshing correctly
2. They reviewed the git diff showing changes to WantedPosterManager.ts
3. The bug was that SetWantedPosterData was being called before node.active = true, which caused skeleton render data to not rebuild
4. They reordered the operations in three methods to activate the node first, then set the data
5. They committed the changes with a detailed commit message
6. They amended the commit to clean up the message formatting
7. The work is complete and committed (SHA 00dacb3) but not pushed yet

I need to summarize this as an observer of that session, not as if I did the work myself.

content.text:
<summary>
  <request>Commit bugfix for WantedPoster skeleton skin rendering issue in uk_pirates_queen Cocos project</request>
  <investigated>Reviewed git diff showing changes to WantedPosterManager.ts where three methods (ShowFirstBounty, PlayBounties callback, and EnterAt) had their operation order modified. Examined the root cause where BaseSymbol 9 was incorrectly displaying as 1 on the first hand but correctly on subsequent hands.</investigated>
  <learned>Cocos Creator's spine skeleton system has a critical gotcha: calling setSkin() on an inactive node doesn't rebuild render data for already-rendered skeletons, causing old skin graphics to persist. The correct pattern is to activate the node first (node.active = true), then call setSkin/SetWantedPosterData, ensuring the skeleton's render data is properly rebuilt with the new skin.</learned>
  <completed>Fixed skeleton skin rendering bug in WantedPosterManager.ts by reordering operations in three methods to activate nodes before setting skin data. Committed changes with detailed explanation (SHA 00dacb3, 1 file changed, 6 insertions, 3 deletions). Verified fix with device logs and screenshots confirming M9+M7 display correctly. Commit message amended to remove formatting artifacts.</completed>
  <next_steps>Changes are committed to main branch but not pushed. Awaiting decision on whether to push, and whether to record the Cocos skeleton rendering gotcha as a reusable pattern for future reference.</next_steps>
  <notes>This fix addresses a subtle rendering engine behavior that affects pooled/recycled UI items with spine skeletons. The bug manifested specifically on first-hand display because intro animations left M1 skeleton render data that wasn't cleared when the node was inactive during skin changes.</notes>
</summary>
